content: This systematic review evaluates the health benefits and risks of intermittent fasting (IF) for adults with metabolic syndrome, drawing on the pooled short-term trial base, pragmatic community evidence, and the methodological context framing the field. Across short-term intermittent energy-restriction trials pooling 317 adults with overweight or obesity aged 21-69 [3], IF produces clinically meaningful weight management benefit. A complementary pragmatic randomized controlled trial in 54 adults with baseline eating durations greater than 14 hours demonstrated that a 12-hour time-restricted eating window delivers metabolic benefit in a real-world population with feasible adherence requirements [10]. The alternate-day fasting variant has separately been pooled across seven trials enrolling 269 adults aged 18-70 [3], establishing ADF as a characterized variant within the broader IF family. The retrieved evidence supports directionally favorable short-term intermediate-endpoint outcomes and distinguishes caloric-restriction from timing-modification as conceptually separable mechanisms [9]. Long-duration prospective cohort data linking IF to hard cardiovascular endpoints are not anchored in the retrieved claim set, and the long-term cardiovascular safety question therefore remains genuinely open. IF is a defensible short-term behavioral intervention for motivated adults with metabolic syndrome, subject to conservative communication about unresolved long-horizon questions.
